title: India and Nepal Broaden Bilateral Agenda Beyond Energy Into AI, Startups, Renewable Energy
lede: External Affairs Minister S. Jaishankar met Nepal's Foreign Minister Shisir Khanal on June 6, 2026, signaling a strategic pivot to expand India-Nepal cooperation into emerging technology sectors while maintaining traditional anchors in energy, trade, and security.

sections.context: The meeting between EAM Jaishankar and FM Khanal reflects a deliberate effort to deepen India-Nepal bilateral engagement across multiple domains. Jaishankar emphasized that India-Nepal relations rest on 'a strong foundation of vibrant people-to-people ties, cross-border connect and shared cultural and religious traditions,' anchored by 'shared trust, goodwill and mutual benefit.' The diplomat noted that existing cooperation spans 'trade, commerce, investments, energy, development cooperation, education, disaster response and culture,' but the June 6 meeting introduced explicit focus on 'newer domains like startups, AI, Information Technology, renewable energy, and others.' This widening of the bilateral agenda suggests both countries recognize the need to modernize their partnership beyond traditional sectors. The timing reflects broader efforts to institutionalize the relationship at multiple levels and strengthen institutional mechanisms.
sections.threads: This expansion aligns with [[india-np-india-and-nepal-pivot-bilateral-ties-beyond-energy-to-a-2026|India and Nepal's broader pivot away from energy-centric cooperation toward technology and innovation]]. The emphasis on renewable energy and AI suggests Delhi is attempting to position Nepal within its broader digital and clean energy ecosystems, while maintaining the critical relationship that has historically been anchored by hydropower agreements such as [[india-np-india-locks-in-1800-mw-of-nepali-hydropower-the-eastern-grid-2018|India's 1,800 MW hydropower lock-in from 2018]]. The meeting also reflects India's commitment to regional stability: Jaishankar specifically noted that 'in the wake of the ongoing crisis in West Asia, the Middle East, we have ensured uninterrupted fuel supplies to Nepal,' demonstrating how India leverages economic interdependence to strengthen political alignment during global crises. The institutional deepening being pursued complements earlier frameworks, as evidenced by [[nepal-fms-delhi-visit-institutionalizes-criminal-justic-2026|Nepal FM's prior visit which institutionalized criminal justice cooperation]].
sections.signal: Analysts should monitor whether India-Nepal bilateral cooperation mechanisms can translate these announced priority areas—startups, AI, renewable energy—into concrete institutional frameworks and funding commitments over the next 12-18 months. The mention of 'newer domains' without detailed specifications suggests ongoing negotiations; watch for bilateral agreements, MoUs, or institutional bodies dedicated to these sectors. The emphasis on maintaining 'security along our long and uniquely open border' while expanding into technology cooperation indicates Delhi is attempting to compartmentalize bilateral ties—expanding economic and technological interdependence while managing border sensitivities. Given Nepal's historical navigation between Indian and Chinese spheres of influence, India's pivot to higher-technology and higher-value cooperation may be designed to deepen Nepal's integration into Indian economic ecosystems before Beijing counters with its own technology and infrastructure offers. The next decision point will be whether these announcements translate into resource allocation in India's upcoming development assistance budgets and whether Nepal reciprocates by institutionalizing these frameworks through parliamentary or cabinet-level agreements.
